patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs readahead function to release extent locks per folio, rather than holding them for the entire readahead loop. This change is intended to prevent a race condition between readahead and direct IO writes that was introduced in kernel version 6.14. The patch reverts the behavior of locking/unlocking extents as a unit around the readahead loop, which was changed in kernel version 6.12. By releasing extent locks per folio, overlapping reads can proceed without blocking on the extent lock.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the patch does not address a potential issue where compressed readahead can expand the RA range, resulting in folios without extent locks, but assured that direct reads on compressed extents will always fallback to buffered reads and thus there is no window to race with direct reads.
review_comments.raw_body:
Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>


Just to mention that Sashiko gave a review with level high, which 
doesn't seem to be caused by this patch itself, and seems to be a false 
alert.

Btrfs compressed readahead can expand the RA range, resulting folios 
that has no extent lock at all.
Thus in theory we still can have some folios without extent lock for 
compressed readahead.

But thankfully direct read on compressed extent will always fallback to 
buffered read, thus there is no window to race with direct reads.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that unlocking the extent lock for a folio in readahead can still cause issues if other threads are holding locks on overlapping ranges, and suggested adding an extra check to ensure the unlock is only done within the original readahead range.
review_comments.raw_body:
Wait, this is still problematic, that we can clear the EXTENT_LOCK for 
ranges that is held by other threads.

The read itself is protected by folio lock so we won't race, but some 
other readers may got their extent lock unlocked still seems problematic.

Can we do an extra check on the folio range and only unlock if it's 
inside the original RA range?

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fallback to transaction csum tree on a commit root csum miss

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fallback to transaction csum tree on a commit root csum miss

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a fallback mechanism to the transaction csum tree when there's a commit root csum miss in btrfs. It addresses an issue where relocation operations don't update the extent generation, leading to zero csum errors. The patch adds a generic fallback to try again with the transaction csum root after a relocation transaction.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 1/3] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v7 1/3] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series removes an unnecessary include of linux/sched.h from the tracing/lock code. The change is made by simply removing the offending line, as none of the trace events in lock.h reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 3/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v7 3/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking mechanism. It fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, and rwsems, to enable this tracepoint. The placement of the tracepoint varies depending on the lock type, but it generally fires before the lock is released or after the release but before the wake. This patch also adds support for reader/writer locks, firing the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting.

The patch also addresses issues with existing contention_begin/contention_end tracepoints, which only work for certain types of locks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 00/21] nfsd: more bugfixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/21] nfsd: clear opcnt on compound arg release to prevent OOB read
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where stale compound metadata could be exposed to userspace through the status interface. The problem occurred when an error path was taken after setting the operation count (opcnt) to an odd value, causing an out-of-bounds read from adjacent slab memory. To fix this, the patch clears the opcnt field unconditionally in nfsd4_release_compoundargs().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/21] nfsd: add missing read barrier to rpc_status_get dumpit seqcount retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a missing read memory barrier to the rpc_status_get_dumpit function in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(). The barrier ensures that all data reads complete before re-checking the status counter, preventing potential issues on weakly-ordered architectures. This fix is necessary because the hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in this function lacks a read memory barrier, which can lead to inconsistent results and ineffective retry logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/21] nfsd: fix netlink dumpit error handling for rpc_status_get
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an error handling issue in the NFS daemon's netlink dump functionality for rpc_status_get. When nla_put operations fail, a partial message is left in the skb without canceling it, causing the netlink dump infrastructure to treat it as a fatal error and abort the entire dump. The patch corrects this by canceling the partial message on all nla_put failure paths and paginating when possible rather than returning a fatal error.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/21] sunrpc: defer rq_argp and rq_resp free until after RCU grace period
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free issue in the sunrpc code by deferring the free of rq_argp and rq_resp until after an RCU grace period, ensuring that RCU readers do not access freed resources. The patch introduces a new callback function svc_rqst_free_rcu to handle the delayed freeing of these resources, while synchronously freeing other resources such as bvec, buffer pages, scratch folio, and auth_data.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/21] nfsd: check nfsd4_acl_to_attr() return value in nfsd4_create()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where ACL translation errors were being silently discarded during file creation. The patch adds an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr() to ensure that any errors are properly handled and do not get overwritten by subsequent code. This fix ensures that ACLs are correctly translated and applied during file creation, preventing potential security issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/21] nfsd: add filehandle match check to nfsd4_delegreturn()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a filehandle match check to the nfsd4_delegreturn() function, which is a stateful NFSv4 operation that previously did not verify the delegation's file matches cstate->current_fh. This fix prevents clients from DELEGRETURNing with a mismatched filehandle, which could destroy the correct delegation and wake up the wrong inode's waiters.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/21] nfsd: validate nseconds in TIME_DELEG decode paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds range checks to prevent a malformed timespec from being stored in the Linux kernel's NFS server implementation. The checks are added to two files, nfs4xdr.c and nfs4callback.c, which handle SETATTR and CB_GETATTR paths respectively. The checks ensure that the nseconds value is less than NSEC_PER_SEC, preventing potential issues with notify_change() propagating malformed timespeces to disk.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/21] nfsd: remove premature NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED in CLAIM_PREVIOUS path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes a premature setting of NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED in the CLAIM_PREVIOUS path, which can lead to incorrect behavior when an open fails. The correct success-path setter is already present in init_open_stateid(). This change affects only NFSv4.1+ sessions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/21] nfsd: fix version mismatch loops in nfsd_acl_init_request()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_acl_init_request function where it incorrectly checks for supported versions by using rqstp->rq_vers instead of the loop variable i, causing an infinite loop and incorrect results. The fix involves replacing rqstp->rq_vers with i to correctly determine the supported version range. This patch addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit that allowed containers to set supported NFS versions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/21] nfsd: fix FL_SLEEP being set unconditionally for all LOCK types
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where the FL_SLEEP flag was being set unconditionally for all lock types, including non-blocking ones. The fix replaces the bitmask test with explicit equality checks to ensure that FL_SLEEP is only set when necessary. This addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit and improves the accuracy of lock notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/21] nfsd: add fh_want_write() for early-verified SETATTR in nfsd_proc_setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a call to fh_want_write() in the nfsd_proc_setattr() function, specifically after an early verification of the file handle using fh_verify(). This ensures that notify_change() is called with a mount write reference, which was previously skipped due to the early verification. The fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch that disabled a timestamp workaround for NFSv3+.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/21] nfsd: fix clock domain mismatch in clients_still_reclaiming()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a clock domain mismatch in the clients_still_reclaiming() function of the NFS server. The function was using CLOCK_REALTIME to compute a deadline, but comparing it against CLOCK_BOOTTIME, which is always false due to the large difference between these two clocks. To fix this, the patch adds a new boot_time_bt field that uses CLOCK_BOOTTIME and updates the deadline computation to use this new field. This prevents clients from holding the server in grace indefinitely by sending CLAIM_PREVIOUS OPEN requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/21] nfsd: use test_and_clear_bit for somebody_reclaimed to prevent lost update
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem in the NFS daemon where a concurrent update could be lost due to a race condition between testing and clearing a flag. The patch replaces separate test_bit() and clear_bit() calls with a single atomic test_and_clear_bit() operation, ensuring that updates are not lost.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 14/21] nfsd: reject reclaim LOCK after RECLAIM_COMPLETE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 server where it incorrectly accepts reclaim LOCK requests after RECLAIM_COMPLETE has been sent by a client. The fix adds a check to ensure that the client's NFSD4_CLIENT_RECLAIM_COMPLETE bit is set before accepting a reclaim request, as required by RFC 8881. This prevents the server from accepting invalid reclaim requests when the grace period is still active.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 15/21] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a bug in the NFS daemon's netlink listener set functionality, where an invalid sockaddr length can cause an out-of-bounds read. The fix involves tightening the policy for sockaddr lengths and adding per-family validation to prevent such issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 16/21] lockd, nfsd: RCU-protect nlmsvc_ops dispatch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free bug in the lockd and nfsd modules by RCU-protecting the nlmsvc_ops dispatch. The bug occurs when the nfsd module is removed, causing stale loads of nlmsvc_ops and resulting in either a NULL dereference or a module-text use-after-free. To fix this, the patch declares nlmsvc_ops as __rcu, publishes it via rcu_assign_pointer(), and clears it via RCU_INIT_POINTER() + synchronize_rcu(). Additionally, a struct module *owner field is added to nlmsvc_binding and the module is pinned across indirect calls with try_module_get/module_put. When the binding is torn down, fput() is used to avoid leaking struct file references.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 17/21] nfsd: move nfsd_debugfs_init() after nfsd4_init_slabs() in init_nfsd()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the initialization of NFS debugfs to after slab allocation in the init_nfsd function, ensuring that debugfs files are properly cleaned up when slab allocation fails. The patch also updates the unwind paths for both the init_nfsd error path and exit_nfsd function to match reverse-initialization order, which is a cleanup with no functional change.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/21] nfsd: initialize DRC hash table before registering shrinker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server code where the DRC hash table is not fully initialized before it becomes visible to the shrinker, causing a NULL dereference on weakly-ordered architectures. The fix involves moving the initialization of the bucket list heads and the drc_hashsize store before registering the shrinker. This ensures that the hash table is properly initialized before it can be accessed by the shrinker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 19/21] nfsd: restore rq_status_counter to even on all nfsd_dispatch() exit paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch restores the rq_status_counter to an even value on all exit paths of nfsd_dispatch(), ensuring that a lockless reader can detect concurrent mutations in request fields. The counter is initially set to an odd value when a request is decoded, and then reset to an even value after processing. However, previous code only restored the counter to an even value on the fully successful path, leaving it stuck at an odd value on other paths. This patch adds a helper function to advance the counter to the next even value and calls it on every return path that follows the initial odd-valued store. The decode-error path is left untouched as it occurs before the counter is set odd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 20/21] nfsd: reset thread skip index when advancing pools in rpc_status dumpit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the NFS daemon where when resuming a netlink dump of rpc_status, the thread skip count was applied to every pool, not just the current one. As a result, some threads were silently omitted from the dump. The patch resets the thread skip index only for the current pool and starts each subsequent pool from thread 0, ensuring that all threads are included in the dump.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 21/21] nfsd: drop the stateid, not the stateowner, on seqid_op replay retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 state management code where an incorrect reference was being dropped, leading to a potential use-after-free and refcount underflow issue. The fix involves dropping the correct reference (the stid) instead of the stateowner when retrying a replay operation. This ensures that the stateowner remains alive through its reference held by the stid, mirroring the behavior in the open path.

patches_submitted.patch_summary:
This version is addresses review comments from Sashiko. There wasn't much in the way of regressions in v1, but it did notice a number of nearby problems that should also be fixed.

This adds 3 patches to fix those. I also dropped the localio patch from the series since that should probably go through the NFS client tree. I'll send it separately.

AFAICT, these are garden-variety bugs. Chuck, please consider these for v7.3.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8] array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+ compounds). If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8]
array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves
args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+
compounds).

If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when
nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC
status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
Since iops only has 8 elements and is the last field in struct
nfsd4_compoundargs, reading indices 8-15 accesses adjacent slab memory
and leaks it to userspace via netlink.

Zero opcnt unconditionally in nfsd4_release_compoundargs() so stale
compound metadata is never exposed through the status interface.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit() is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb() to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.
review_comments.raw_body:
The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()
is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter
check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb()
to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.

Without this barrier, on weakly-ordered architectures (ARM, POWER),
the CPU may reorder field reads past the second counter check, making
the retry logic ineffective: it could observe a consistent counter pair
while reading fields that have been concurrently modified by the writer.

Add smp_rmb() before the second smp_load_acquire() to match the
barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.

Fixes: ac18892ea3f7 ("NFSD: add rpc_status netlink support")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c | 6 ++++--
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
index c06d25c06f06..a4b5b1467fe2 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
@@ -1576,9 +1576,11 @@ int nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
 #endif /* CONFIG_NFSD_V4 */
 
 			/*
-			 * Acquire rq_status_counter before reporting the rqst
-			 * fields to the user.
+			 * Ensure all field reads complete before re-checking
+			 * the status counter. Pairs with the smp_store_release
+			 * in nfsd_dispatch to form a seq-lock like protocol.
 			 */
+			smp_rmb();
 			if (smp_load_acquire(&rqstp->rq_status_counter) !=
 			    status_counter)
 				continue;

--

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd_genl_rpc_status_compose_msg() returns -ENOBUFS on nla_put failure without calling genlmsg_cancel(), leaving a partial message in the skb. The caller then propagates -ENOBUFS directly, which the netlink dump infrastructure treats as a fatal error, aborting the entire dump. The correct netlink dump convention is: - Cancel any partial message with genlmsg_cancel() - If prior messages were added to the skb (skb->len > 0), save the current iterator position and return skb->len to paginate - Only return a negative errno when no messages fit at all Fix compose_msg to cancel the partial message on all nla_put failure paths, and fix the caller to paginate when possible rather than returning a fatal error. Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8 --- fs/nfsd/nfsctl.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: svc_rqst_free() frees rqstp->rq_argp and rqstp->rq_resp synchronously via kfree(), but defers the rqstp struct free via kfree_rcu().  After svc_exit_thread() calls list_del_rcu() and svc_rqst_free(), there is a window where RCU readers that started before list_del_rcu() can still traverse the thread list and find the rqstp.  These readers (e.g. nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()) dereference rqstp->rq_argp, which has already been freed \u2014 a use-after-free. Fix this by moving the kfree of rq_argp and rq_resp into an explicit call_rcu() callback alongside the struct free.

not call nfs4_check_fh() to verify the delegation's file matches

filehandle, destroying the correct delegation but waking the wrong

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/20] nfsd: check fl_lmops in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd_breaker_owns_lease() function to quickly check if a lease is an NFS daemon (nfsd) lease by verifying that its fl_lmops field matches the nfsd_lease_mng_ops operations. This optimization allows for earlier rejection of non-nfsd leases, improving performance and reducing unnecessary checks. The patch adds a new constant nfsd_lease_mng_ops to the code and updates the function to check for this value before proceeding with further lease verification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/20] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds protocol support for CB_NOTIFY in the nfsd kernel module. It updates the xdr (External Data Representation) files to include the necessary definitions and removes duplicate definitions from other headers. The patch also regenerates the xdr files, which caused conflicts with constants defined in the RFC but not part of the kernel API. These constants are removed, and userspace consumers should obtain them from more authoritative sources.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the value of NFS4ERR_EOF can be leaked onto the wire, and suggested keeping the .x document aligned with the specification by defining a 'maximum value' symbolic constant in one of the hand-rolled headers instead of copying it from existing definitions. He also pointed out that NFS4ERR_FIRST_FREE is used to determine the numeric value for NFSERR_EOF.

Reviewer suggested moving 'pragma public' directives to where they're first needed, as their necessity may disappear in subsequent patches.
review_comments.raw_body:
This value can be leaked onto the wire. The basic enum encoder
checks that these values are part of the .x before sticking
them on the wire.

Please keep the .x document aligned with the specification. If
you need a "maximum value" symbolic constant, please define it
in one of the hand-rolled headers. (I guess this one was copied
over from the existing hand-rolled NFS4ERR definitions).

I see that NFS4ERR_FIRST_FREE is used to determine the numeric
value for NFSERR_EOF.

fs/nfsd/nfs3xdr.c:              if (xdr_stream_encode_bool(xdr, resp->common.err == nfserr_eof) < 0)
fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c:      return nfsd4_encode_bool(xdr, readdir->common.err == nfserr_eof);
fs/nfsd/nfsd.h: __be32                  err;    /* 0, nfserr, or nfserr_eof */
fs/nfsd/nfsd.h:#define  nfserr_eof              cpu_to_be32(NFSERR_EOF)
fs/nfsd/nfsxdr.c:               if (xdr_stream_encode_bool(xdr, resp->common.err == nfserr_eof) < 0)
fs/nfsd/vfs.c:          cdp->err = nfserr_eof; /* will be cleared on successful read */
fs/nfsd/vfs.c:  if (err == nfserr_eof || err == nfserr_toosmall)

A better interim approach might be to select an impossible value
for NFSERR_EOF, as is done for the internal NLM error status codes:

fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__drop_reply   cpu_to_be32(30000)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__deadlock     cpu_to_be32(30001)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__stale_fh     cpu_to_be32(30002)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__failed       cpu_to_be32(30003)

---

Let's add the "pragma public" directives in the patches where
they are first needed, instead of here. As subsequent patches
are modified, the need for these directives might vanish.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/20] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new flags to the NFSv4 protocol, specifically NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis. These flags are intended to be used by later patches and allow Linux nfsd to omit certain information about directory cookies and ordering when providing a GET_DIR_DELEGATION response. The changes are based on a working group document that has been stable for over a year, but there is still some risk associated with the addition of these new flags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/20] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS server to request a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to selectively ignore certain events such as file creation, deletion, and renaming. The patch introduces a new function `nfsd_notify_to_ignore` that converts notification types into an ignore mask, which is then applied to the file lease flags. This change enables more fine-grained control over the notifications provided to clients, addressing a limitation in the previous implementation where all events were always reported.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/20] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a directory delegation in the NFS daemon. It introduces a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(), which recalculates the fsnotify mask based on the leases present. The function is called when directory delegations are added or removed and ensures that concurrent calls to this function do not interfere with each other by using a mutex to serialize access to the lease state and mark update. This patch addresses potential issues where a recalculation working from stale data could clobber a concurrent update, leaving the mark missing required events.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/20] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether callback processing should continue. The change is made to handle allocation failures during CB_NOTIFY operations, where processing should stop if an allocation fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/20] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds encoding and decoding routines for CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFSv4 callback protocol. The encoding routine uses code generated by xdrgen to encode the message, while the decoding routine decodes the message using existing functions. Currently, the encoder is a stub that will be fleshed out in later patches. This patch also updates the nfsd state header and adds a new procedure for CB_NOTIFY to the NFSv4 callback procedures array.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/20] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch protects the fi_deleg_file pointer in the NFS server code using RCU (Read-Copy Update) to prevent concurrent access issues during delegation teardown and lease removal. The patch annotates fi_deleg_file with __rcu, converts all accessors to use proper RCU primitives, and introduces rcu_dereference_protected() for safe dereferencing under certain conditions. This change prepares the code for a subsequent patch that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/20] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and callback operations to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces two new structs: nfsd_notify_event, which carries fsnotify events for CB_NOTIFY, and nfsd4_cb_notify, which represents per-delegation state for notification handling. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg() into a common function with a pluggable sc_free callback and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg(), which allocates resources needed for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it initializes the nfsd4_cb_notify_ops structure with done and release handlers that will be filled in when the notification path is implemented.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/20] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds notification handlers for directory events in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces a new fsnotify group for directories and modifies existing code to use this new group when handling directory-related events. The patch also creates a CB_NOTIFY request for directory change events and allocates a nfsd_notify_event object to handle notifications. If any delegation is affected by the event, it recalls the delegation if necessary. This patch aims to improve the NFS server's ability to notify clients about changes to directories.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() may return true even if ncn_encode_err is still set, leading to unnecessary recall of delegations in nfsd_break_one_deleg().

Reviewer noted that nfsd_break_one_deleg() unconditionally increments the refcount of a delegation's sc_count, creating a potential inc-from-zero issue when called from nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() without holding the flc_lock. The reviewer suggested using refcount_inc_not_zero to guard against this and pointed out a similar correction made in the last patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
This flag is zeroed only once, at allocation time in alloc_init_dir_deleg().
It is never cleared in nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare().

Since nfsd4_cb_notify_release() can requeue the callback (via
nfsd4_run_cb_notify) when events arrive while a callback is in flight,
->prepare may encode cleanly and return true, but nfsd4_cb_notify_done()
still observes the stale ncn_encode_err == true and calls
nfsd_break_one_deleg() -- discarding a good notification and recalling
the delegation unnecessarily.

---

nfsd_break_one_deleg() does an unconditional
refcount_inc(&dp->dl_stid.sc_count), and its comment justifies this
with "the lease code is serialized by the flc_lock." That invariant
holds when called from nfsd_break_deleg_cb() under flc_lock, but
nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() runs on a workqueue WITHOUT flc_lock. Its
out_recall: path calls nfsd_break_one_deleg(dp)
directly. The delegation can be concurrently destroyed with sc_count
already at zero, making this an inc-from-zero.

The dispatch path nfsd4_run_cb_notify already does this correctly with
refcount_inc_not_zero. The out_recall path needs the same guard (skip
the recall / bail if the refcount is already zero).

I notice that the last unapplied patch ("nfsd: add
support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes") rewrites the guard
"if (count > NOTIFY4_EVENT_QUEUE_SIZE)" into "if (count > limit)" with
limit = NOTIFY4_EVENT_QUEUE_SIZE - 1 when NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS is
requested. That turns the previously-dead overflow branch into a live,
routine path to out_recall, which adds another normal-operation route
into this unlocked recall.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that nfsd_break_one_deleg() should handle a delegations refcount being zero by skipping the recall callback, and plans to add a refcount_inc_not_zero() in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
This wart has been there a long time, and we just papered over it with
the lock.

I think we need to do a refcount_inc_not_zero() in
nfsd_break_one_deleg() and just return without queuing the callback if
it's already at 0. That means that the recall is racing with the lease
teardown, so I think the right thing to do is to not send the recall in
that case.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 11/20] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the NFSv4 server implementation, specifically related to delegation and notification handling. The current code does not properly handle notifications for order-aware clients, leading to potential issues with directory cache ordering. This patch aims to correct this by applying the notify mask to the delegation when requested.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a flaw in the patch's handling of notifications for order-aware clients, which could lead to issues with directory cache ordering. Noted that this issue is latent when the patch is first applied but will become apparent with future order-aware clients.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026, at 1:50 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> If the client requests a directory delegation with notifications
> enabled, set the appropriate return mask in gddr_notification[0]. This
> will ensure the lease acquisition sets the appropriate ignore mask.
>
> If the client doesn't set NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, then don't offer any
> notifications, as nfsd won't provide directory offset information, and
> "classic" notifications require them.
>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c | 12 ++++++++++++
>  1 file changed, 12 insertions(+)
>
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> index 0c37d7c6d28c..29f7339dc220 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> @@ -2530,12 +2530,18 @@ nfsd4_verify(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct 
> nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  	return status == nfserr_same ? nfs_ok : status;
>  }
> 
> +#define SUPPORTED_NOTIFY_MASK	(BIT(NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY) |	\
> +				 BIT(NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY) |	\
> +				 BIT(NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY) |	\
> +				 BIT(NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND))
> +
>  static __be32
>  nfsd4_get_dir_delegation(struct svc_rqst *rqstp,
>  			 struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  			 union nfsd4_op_u *u)
>  {
>  	struct nfsd4_get_dir_delegation *gdd = &u->get_dir_delegation;
> +	u32 requested = gdd->gdda_notification_types[0];
>  	struct nfs4_delegation *dd;
>  	struct nfsd_file *nf;
>  	__be32 status;
> @@ -2544,6 +2550,12 @@ nfsd4_get_dir_delegation(struct svc_rqst *rqstp,
>  	if (status != nfs_ok)
>  		return status;
> 
> +	/* No notifications if you don't set NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND! */
> +	if (!(requested & BIT(NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND)))
> +		requested = 0;
> +
> +	gdd->gddr_notification[0] = requested & SUPPORTED_NOTIFY_MASK;
> +
>  	/*
>  	 * RFC 8881, section 18.39.3 says:
>  	 *
>

When a client requests NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND | NOTIFY4_CFLAG_ORDER
plus ADD/REMOVE/RENAME, the assignment still grants the content
notification bits because it only requires GFLAG_EXTEND. The rest
of NFSD's CB_NOTIFY encoder does not store that order-aware request
and emits zero/absent cookie and previous-entry information, which
is only safe for order-unaware clients.

An order-aware client can then keep an ordered directory cache from
unusable notifications instead of having the delegation recalled.

The bis draft requires order info for order-aware clients, or recall:

   - 27.4.5 (REMOVE): "If the client is order-aware, the server will send
     the cookie value as part of this."
   - 16.2.13: order-aware == NOTIFY4_CFLAG_ORDER set OR NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND
     reset.
   - 16.2.11.3: "If the client is concerned with entry order and these
     notifications ... cannot be sent for any other reason, then the
     delegation is recalled."

This patch's own first rule (drop everything when GFLAG_EXTEND is
reset) is exactly the order-aware -> no-notifications principle
for legacy RFC8881 clients. It misses the parallel case:
GFLAG_EXTEND set PLUS CFLAG_ORDER set is also order-aware.

This finding is latent when this patch is first applied, but the
NFSD's negotiation is incorrect for any future order-aware client.

I don't see this issue addressed by a subsequent patch in this
series.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 12/20] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `nfsd4_encode_attr_vals` to the NFS server code, which marshals attribute values from completed arguments into a JSON object. The function breaks out the encoding of actual attr_vals field into a separate function, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 13/20] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change function to work even when there is no export available, which can occur in certain CB_NOTIFY callback scenarios. The change ensures that a delegation will not be granted on the V4 root, making this safe. This modification enables the function to operate correctly without requiring access to an svc_export.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 14/20] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic inode information in a CB_NOTIFY event, including file attributes that can be acquired via GETATTR. The patch modifies the nfsd4_setup_notify_entry4 function to include inode attributes in the notify entry. It uses the vfs_getattr function to retrieve the inode attributes and then encodes them into the notify entry using the nfsd4_encode_attr_vals function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 15/20] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces changes to the NFSv4 server (nfsd) to allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. The current implementation requires a svc_fh, which is not available in certain callback scenarios such as CB_NOTIFY. To address this issue, a new 'fhandle' field is added to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and the filehandle is copied from the svc_fh into this field. Additionally, the fh_append_mac() function is generalized to operate on a bare knfsd_fh rather than a svc_fh.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with FSID encoding in the CB_NOTIFY path after this patch is applied, where nfsd4_encode_fattr4_fsid() calls fsid_source(args->fhp) without a NULL check. Suggested adding a NULL check to prevent dereferencing a NULL fhp.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 16/20] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, fi_connectable, to the nfs4_file structure in the NFSv4 state management code. The flag indicates whether a filehandle is connectable or not, which is necessary for encoding child filehandles in CB_NOTIFY operations.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a problem where fi_connectable depends on the export that initialized the shared object, leading to incorrect connectability in CB_NOTIFY operations. Suggested using the delegation's sc_export instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026, at 1:50 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> When encoding a filehandle for a CB_NOTIFY, there is no svc_export
> available, but the server needs to know whether to encode a connectable
> filehandle. Add a flag to the nfs4_file that tells whether the
> svc_export under which a directory delegation was acquired has subtree
> checking enabled, in which case it needs connectable filehandles.
>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c | 1 +
>  fs/nfsd/state.h     | 1 +
>  2 files changed, 2 insertions(+)
>
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> index 513cbc1a583f..aa99783ce901 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> @@ -5231,6 +5231,7 @@ static void nfsd4_file_init(const struct svc_fh 
> *fh, struct nfs4_file *fp)
>  	memset(fp->fi_access, 0, sizeof(fp->fi_access));
>  	fp->fi_aliased = false;
>  	fp->fi_inode = d_inode(fh->fh_dentry);
> +	fp->fi_connectable = !(fh->fh_export->ex_flags & 
> NFSEXP_NOSUBTREECHECK);
>  #ifdef CONFIG_NFSD_PNFS
>  	INIT_LIST_HEAD(&fp->fi_lo_states);
>  	atomic_set(&fp->fi_lo_recalls, 0);
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/state.h b/fs/nfsd/state.h
> index f8457e0f2b57..d912e3d04dd7 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/state.h
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/state.h
> @@ -761,6 +761,7 @@ struct nfs4_file {
>  	int			fi_delegees;
>  	struct knfsd_fh		fi_fhandle;
>  	bool			fi_had_conflict;
> +	bool			fi_connectable;
>  #ifdef CONFIG_NFSD_PNFS
>  	struct list_head	fi_lo_states;
>  	atomic_t		fi_lo_recalls;
>

When two clients use exports of the same directory root that
differ only in subtree_check/no_subtree_check, the root filehandle
is the same and nfsd4_file_hash_insert() can reuse the same
nfs4_file. This makes fi_connectable depend on whichever export
first initialized the shared object, so a later directory
delegation acquired under the other export can encode CB_NOTIFY
child filehandles with the wrong connectability.

Therefore, the delegation's sc_export is the per-export state to
derive connectability from, and the export is already available
via dp->dl_stid.sc_export.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 17/20] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for returning the filehandle in CB_NOTIFY operations, which is necessary because nfsd's usual fh_compose routine requires a svc_export and fills out a svc_fh, but there is no such export available in the context of a CB_NOTIFY. A new routine is added to compose a filehandle with only a parent filehandle and nfs4_file, and this is used to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the constructed filehandle attribute in CB_NOTIFY operations, where it may not be properly signed or MACed. Recommended either signing the constructed handle or suppressing the attribute when the export requires signed filehandles.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 18/20] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of child attributes in NFSv4 delegation by tracking the union of requested and supported attributes, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This ensures that the client receives accurate information about the file system's capabilities and limitations. The patch modifies several files to implement this change, including nfsd/nfs4proc.c, nfsd/nfs4state.c, and nfsd/nfs4xdr.c.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that when a client requests any supported child attribute in word 1, it can make gddr_child_attributes[1] non-zero, leading to nfsd4_encode_bitmap4() emitting a two-word bitmap, and nfsd4_get_dir_delegation_rsize() still budgeting only the old one-word child-attribute bitmap, resulting in NFS4ERR_RESOURCE/REP_TOO_BIG errors when encoding fails near the reply/slot limit.
review_comments.raw_body:
When a client requests any supported child attribute in word 1, this can
make gddr_child_attributes[1] non-zero, so nfsd4_encode_bitmap4() emits a
two-word bitmap. nfsd4_get_dir_delegation_rsize() still budgets only the
old one-word child-attribute bitmap before executing this non-idempotent
op, so a compound near the reply/slot limit can grant a directory
delegation and then fail encoding with NFS4ERR_RESOURCE/REP_TOO_BIG,
leaving the client without the returned stateid.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 19/20] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new feature to track the union of requested and supported directory attributes in the delegation, which will be used later to ensure that only relevant attributes are encoded when sending updates.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's impact on bisectability, specifically that it allows clients to request NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS without actually being able to send dir-attribute notifications.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 11, 2026, at 1:50 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Track the union of requested and supported dir attributes in the
> delegation. In a later patch this will be used to ensure that we
> only encode the attributes in that union when sending
> add/remove/rename updates.
>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c  |  9 ++++++---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c | 20 ++++++++++++++++----
>  fs/nfsd/state.h     |  2 ++
>  3 files changed, 24 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> index caec82e77081..9e86f5907f06 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> @@ -2530,9 +2530,10 @@ nfsd4_verify(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct 
> nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  	return status == nfserr_same ? nfs_ok : status;
>  }
> 
> -#define SUPPORTED_NOTIFY_MASK	(BIT(NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY) |	\
> -				 BIT(NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY) |	\
> -				 BIT(NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY) |	\
> +#define SUPPORTED_NOTIFY_MASK	(BIT(NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS) |	\
> +				 BIT(NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY) |		\
> +				 BIT(NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY) |		\
> +				 BIT(NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY) |		\
>  				 BIT(NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND))
> 
>  static __be32
> @@ -2579,6 +2580,8 @@ nfsd4_get_dir_delegation(struct svc_rqst *rqstp,
>  	memcpy(&gdd->gddr_stateid, &dd->dl_stid.sc_stateid, 
> sizeof(gdd->gddr_stateid));
>  	gdd->gddr_child_attributes[0] = dd->dl_child_attrs[0];
>  	gdd->gddr_child_attributes[1] = dd->dl_child_attrs[1];
> +	gdd->gddr_dir_attributes[0] = dd->dl_dir_attrs[0];
> +	gdd->gddr_dir_attributes[1] = dd->dl_dir_attrs[1];
>  	nfs4_put_stid(&dd->dl_stid);
>  	return nfs_ok;
>  }
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> index 0e6e008c121e..12627afb604f 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
> @@ -9945,6 +9945,15 @@ nfsd4_deleg_getattr_conflict(struct svc_rqst 
> *rqstp, struct dentry *dentry,
>  				 FATTR4_WORD1_TIME_MODIFY |	\
>  				 FATTR4_WORD1_TIME_CREATE)
> 
> +#define GDD_WORD0_DIR_ATTRS	(FATTR4_WORD0_CHANGE |		\
> +				 FATTR4_WORD0_SIZE)
> +
> +#define GDD_WORD1_DIR_ATTRS	(FATTR4_WORD1_NUMLINKS |	\
> +				 FATTR4_WORD1_SPACE_USED |	\
> +				 FATTR4_WORD1_TIME_ACCESS |	\
> +				 FATTR4_WORD1_TIME_METADATA |	\
> +				 FATTR4_WORD1_TIME_MODIFY)
> +
>  /**
>   * nfsd_get_dir_deleg - attempt to get a directory delegation
>   * @cstate: compound state
> @@ -10013,14 +10022,17 @@ nfsd_get_dir_deleg(struct 
> nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  		dp->dl_stid.sc_export =
>  			exp_get(cstate->current_fh.fh_export);
> 
> -	dp->dl_child_attrs[0] = gdd->gdda_child_attributes[0] & GDD_WORD0_CHILD_ATTRS;
> -	dp->dl_child_attrs[1] = gdd->gdda_child_attributes[1] & GDD_WORD1_CHILD_ATTRS;
> -
>  	/*
>  	 * NB: gddr_notification[0] represents the notifications that
>  	 * will be granted to the client
>  	 */
> -	fl = nfs4_alloc_init_lease(dp, gdd->gddr_notification[0]);
> +	dp->dl_notify_mask = gdd->gddr_notification[0];
> +	dp->dl_child_attrs[0] = gdd->gdda_child_attributes[0] & GDD_WORD0_CHILD_ATTRS;
> +	dp->dl_child_attrs[1] = gdd->gdda_child_attributes[1] & GDD_WORD1_CHILD_ATTRS;
> +	dp->dl_dir_attrs[0] = gdd->gdda_dir_attributes[0] & GDD_WORD0_DIR_ATTRS;
> +	dp->dl_dir_attrs[1] = gdd->gdda_dir_attributes[1] & GDD_WORD1_DIR_ATTRS;
> +
> +	fl = nfs4_alloc_init_lease(dp, dp->dl_notify_mask);
>  	if (!fl)
>  		goto out_put_stid;
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/state.h b/fs/nfsd/state.h
> index 0763893bfd48..17be4011740d 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/state.h
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/state.h
> @@ -299,7 +299,9 @@ struct nfs4_delegation {
>  	struct timespec64	dl_ctime;
> 
>  	/* For dir delegations */
> +	uint32_t		dl_notify_mask;
>  	uint32_t		dl_child_attrs[2];
> +	uint32_t		dl_dir_attrs[2];

Nit: Maybe these should be u32. uint32_t is a user space type.


>  };
> 
>  static inline bool deleg_is_read(u32 dl_type)
>

Bisectability: After this patch is applied, a client that requests
NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS now gets that bit echoed in gddr_notification,
but the callback path still only maps/encodes add, remove, and rename
notifications (nfsd_notify_to_ignore(), nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(),
and nfsd4_encode_notify_event() have no dir-attr case). That lets the
server grant a directory delegation while promising dir-attribute
CB_NOTIFYs it cannot send until the follow-up support lands, so this
bit should not be advertised in this patch.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 20/20] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to the NFS server (nfsd) to send directory attribute change notifications to clients when requested. The client must have specifically asked for these notifications, and they are only sent in addition to other events such as add/remove/rename operations. If the export is configured to require signed filehandles, the server will append a MAC to the filehandle before sending it to the client. This ensures that the client can verify the filehandle's integrity.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that when a client requests dir attribute change notifications, the CB_NOTIFY event queue can overflow due to the reserved slot for the dir-attr-change entry, causing the delegation to be recalled without draining the queue and resulting in requeued callbacks.

Reviewer noted that when xdr_reserve_space() succeeds but nfsd4_encode_dir_attr_change() returns NULL, the reserved space for *maskp is not rolled back and *maskp remains uninitialized, potentially leading to a garbage value being transmitted.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the function nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() unconditionally dereferences a pointer to an export object (dp->dl_stid.sc_export) which may be freed concurrently by drop_stid_export(), leading to a use-after-free bug, and requested synchronization or alternative handling.

Reviewer noted that the if statement checks dl_notify_mask again, even though it was already checked by its sole caller, and suggested removing this redundant check.
review_comments.raw_body:
When a client requests NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS, the CB_NOTIFY event
queue can fill to NOTIFY4_EVENT_QUEUE_SIZE (3) events while the consumer
only accepts 2 (it reserves a slot for the dir-attr-change entry). The
resulting overflow path in nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() recalls the
delegation without draining the queue, and nfsd4_cb_notify_release()
then requeues the same callback indefinitely.

---

Nit:

When xdr_reserve_space() for maskp succeeds but nfsd4_encode_dir_attr_change()
returns NULL, the 4-byte reservation is never rolled back and *maskp is never
written, yet the function still takes the success path (return true). Unlike
the child-event loop, this branch does not escalate to error = true.

This is probably benign only because nfs4_xdr_enc_cb_notify re-encodes from
the ncn_nf[] array (and count was not incremented), so the garbage hole is
never transmitted.

---

The function dereferences the new exp parameter unconditionally.

The argument is dp->dl_stid.sc_export, read unlocked at
nfs4xdr.c:4297 and handed down. An in-flight CB_NOTIFY callback holds a
sc_count reference but NOT an export reference. drop_stid_export() can
run concurrently (admin revoke / unexport), NULL sc_export, and drop what
may be the last export reference, freeing the svc_export while the
callback dereferences it.

---

It looks like this if() re-checks dl_notify_mask even though its
sole caller already gated on the identical check.

nfsd4_encode_notify_event() does not repeat its caller's check.
The guard is unreachable from current callers.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, allowing clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing its lease. The patches include fixes for various issues, such as RCU handling and encoding failures, and have been tested with pynfs but require a complete client-side implementation for final testing. The series builds on previous work on directory delegation and adds support for creates, unlinks, and renames, as well as sending updated directory attributes in notifications.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the lease breaker ownership check in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease() by adding a quick check for nfsd leases, which will have their fl_lmops set to nfsd_lease_mng_ops. The patch adds a new constant struct lease_manager_operations and updates the function to return false if the lease manager operations are not equal to nfsd_lease_mng_ops.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about duplicate definitions in nfs4.h and the uapi nfs4 header by removing them and regenerating the xdr files, which caused conflicts with constants defined in include/uapi/linux/nfs4.h that are part of the RFC. The author noted that userspace consumers should obtain these constants from more authoritative sources.
sentiment_signals: userspace consumers should get constants from more authoritative sources

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the stability of RFC8881bis, which is still a working group document and may change. The author acknowledged that there is some risk but hopes that the parts related to directory delegations will remain stable.
review_comments.raw_body:
RFC8881bis adds some new flags to GET_DIR_DELEGATION that later patches
will consume. In particular, Linux nfsd can't easily provide info about
directory cookies and ordering. The new flags allow it to omit that
information.

There is some risk here -- RFC8881bis is still a working group document,
and has been for years. The changes to directory delegations have been
stable for the last year or so however, so the hope is that those parts
won't change (much).

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x    | 14 +++++++++++++-
 fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr_gen.c                | 13 ++++++++++++-
 fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr_gen.h                |  2 +-
 include/linux/sunrpc/xdrgen/nfs4_1.h | 13 ++++++++++++-
 4 files changed, 38 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x b/Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x
index 632f5b579c39..6039eb024e0e 100644

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the FL_IGN_DIR_* bits not being enabled when requesting a directory lease, and explained that they will be set based on the requested notification types in nfsd_get_dir_deleg(). The patch has been updated to include this fix.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about concurrent delegation adds and removes on the same directory causing stale snapshots of lease lists, which could lead to incorrect fsnotify marks. The author added a new mutex (nfm_recalc_mutex) to serialize callers and ensure that the mark is updated correctly. This fix will be included in the next version.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about CB_NOTIFY operations failing due to allocation issues, and responded by modifying the ->prepare callback operation to return true if processing should continue and false otherwise.

review_comments.raw_body:
Add routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages. These call
into the code generated by xdrgen to do the actual encoding and
decoding.

For now, the encoder is a stub. Later patches will flesh out the payload
encoding.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c | 46 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

 3 files changed, 66 insertions(+)

+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c
@@ -887,6 +887,51 @@ static void encode_stateowner(struct xdr_stream *xdr, struct nfs4_stateowner *so
 	xdr_encode_opaque(p, so->so_owner.data, so->so_owner.len);
 }
 
+static void nfs4_xdr_enc_cb_notify(struct rpc_rqst *req,
+				   struct xdr_stream *xdr,
+				   const void *data)
+{
+	const struct nfsd4_callback *cb = data;
+	struct nfs4_cb_compound_hdr hdr = {
+		.ident = 0,
+		.minorversion = cb->cb_clp->cl_minorversion,
+	};
+	struct CB_NOTIFY4args args = { };
+
+	WARN_ON_ONCE(hdr.minorversion == 0);
+
+	encode_cb_compound4args(xdr, &hdr);
+	encode_cb_sequence4args(xdr, cb, &hdr);
+
+	/*
+	 * FIXME: get stateid and fh from delegation. Inline the cna_changes
+	 * buffer, and zero it.
+	 */
+	xdrgen_encode_CB_NOTIFY4args(xdr, &args);
+
+	hdr.nops++;
+	encode_cb_nops(&hdr);
+}
+
+static int nfs4_xdr_dec_cb_notify(struct rpc_rqst *rqstp,
+				  struct xdr_stream *xdr,
+				  void *data)
+{
+	struct nfsd4_callback *cb = data;
+	struct nfs4_cb_compound_hdr hdr;
+	int status;
+
+	status = decode_cb_compound4res(xdr, &hdr);
+	if (unlikely(status))
+		return status;
+
+	status = decode_cb_sequence4res(xdr, cb);
+	if (unlikely(status || cb->cb_seq_status))
+		return status;
+
+	return decode_cb_op_status(xdr, OP_CB_NOTIFY, &cb->cb_status);
+}

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about concurrent access to fi_deleg_file, which can be NULLed by put_deleg_file() during delegation teardown. The author annotated fi_deleg_file with __rcu and converted all accessors to use RCU primitives to prevent dereferencing a NULL pointer in the CB_NOTIFY callback path.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the lack of support for directory delegation CB_NOTIFY callbacks, explaining that they have added the necessary data structures and callback operations to enable this feature.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the NFSERR_EOF value can be leaked onto the wire and suggested defining a 'maximum value' symbolic constant in one of the hand-rolled headers to avoid this issue.

Reviewer Chuck Lever suggested moving 'pragma public' directives to where they are first needed in the patch series, as subsequent patches may modify their necessity.
review_comments.raw_body:
This value can be leaked onto the wire. The basic enum encoder
checks that these values are part of the .x before sticking
them on the wire.

Please keep the .x document aligned with the specification. If
you need a "maximum value" symbolic constant, please define it
in one of the hand-rolled headers. (I guess this one was copied
over from the existing hand-rolled NFS4ERR definitions).

I see that NFS4ERR_FIRST_FREE is used to determine the numeric
value for NFSERR_EOF.

fs/nfsd/nfs3xdr.c:              if (xdr_stream_encode_bool(xdr, resp->common.err == nfserr_eof) < 0)
fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c:      return nfsd4_encode_bool(xdr, readdir->common.err == nfserr_eof);
fs/nfsd/nfsd.h: __be32                  err;    /* 0, nfserr, or nfserr_eof */
fs/nfsd/nfsd.h:#define  nfserr_eof              cpu_to_be32(NFSERR_EOF)
fs/nfsd/nfsxdr.c:               if (xdr_stream_encode_bool(xdr, resp->common.err == nfserr_eof) < 0)
fs/nfsd/vfs.c:          cdp->err = nfserr_eof; /* will be cleared on successful read */
fs/nfsd/vfs.c:  if (err == nfserr_eof || err == nfserr_toosmall)

A better interim approach might be to select an impossible value
for NFSERR_EOF, as is done for the internal NLM error status codes:

fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__drop_reply   cpu_to_be32(30000)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__deadlock     cpu_to_be32(30001)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__stale_fh     cpu_to_be32(30002)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__failed       cpu_to_be32(30003)

---

Let's add the "pragma public" directives in the patches where
they are first needed, instead of here. As subsequent patches
are modified, the need for these directives might vanish.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Chuck Lever noted that nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() does not clear the ncn_encode_err flag, which can lead to unnecessary recall of delegations when events arrive while a callback is in flight.

The reviewer noted that nfsd_break_one_deleg() unconditionally increments the delegation's reference count without checking if it is already zero, which can lead to a lock ordering violation when called from nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare(). The correct approach should be to use refcount_inc_not_zero() as done in nfsd4_run_cb_notify().
review_comments.raw_body:
This flag is zeroed only once, at allocation time in alloc_init_dir_deleg().
It is never cleared in nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare().

Since nfsd4_cb_notify_release() can requeue the callback (via
nfsd4_run_cb_notify) when events arrive while a callback is in flight,
->prepare may encode cleanly and return true, but nfsd4_cb_notify_done()
still observes the stale ncn_encode_err == true and calls
nfsd_break_one_deleg() -- discarding a good notification and recalling
the delegation unnecessarily.

---

nfsd_break_one_deleg() does an unconditional
refcount_inc(&dp->dl_stid.sc_count), and its comment justifies this
with "the lease code is serialized by the flc_lock." That invariant
holds when called from nfsd_break_deleg_cb() under flc_lock, but
nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() runs on a workqueue WITHOUT flc_lock. Its
out_recall: path calls nfsd_break_one_deleg(dp)
directly. The delegation can be concurrently destroyed with sc_count
already at zero, making this an inc-from-zero.

The dispatch path nfsd4_run_cb_notify already does this correctly with
refcount_inc_not_zero. The out_recall path needs the same guard (skip
the recall / bail if the refcount is already zero).

I notice that the last unapplied patch ("nfsd: add
support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes") rewrites the guard
"if (count > NOTIFY4_EVENT_QUEUE_SIZE)" into "if (count > limit)" with
limit = NOTIFY4_EVENT_QUEUE_SIZE - 1 when NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS is
requested. That turns the previously-dead overflow branch into a live,
routine path to out_recall, which adds another normal-operation route
into this unlocked recall.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current implementation of NFSD's CB_NOTIFY encoder does not store order-aware requests and emits zero/absent cookie information, which can lead to unusable notifications for order-aware clients. This is in conflict with the bis draft requirements and will cause delegation recall when an order-aware client tries to keep an ordered directory cache.
review_comments.raw_body:
When a client requests NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND | NOTIFY4_CFLAG_ORDER
plus ADD/REMOVE/RENAME, the assignment still grants the content
notification bits because it only requires GFLAG_EXTEND. The rest
of NFSD's CB_NOTIFY encoder does not store that order-aware request
and emits zero/absent cookie and previous-entry information, which
is only safe for order-unaware clients.

An order-aware client can then keep an ordered directory cache from
unusable notifications instead of having the delegation recalled.

The bis draft requires order info for order-aware clients, or recall:

   - 27.4.5 (REMOVE): "If the client is order-aware, the server will send
     the cookie value as part of this."
   - 16.2.13: order-aware == NOTIFY4_CFLAG_ORDER set OR NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND
     reset.
   - 16.2.11.3: "If the client is concerned with entry order and these
     notifications ... cannot be sent for any other reason, then the
     delegation is recalled."

This patch's own first rule (drop everything when GFLAG_EXTEND is
reset) is exactly the order-aware -> no-notifications principle
for legacy RFC8881 clients. It misses the parallel case:
GFLAG_EXTEND set PLUS CFLAG_ORDER set is also order-aware.

This finding is latent when this patch is first applied, but the
NFSD's negotiation is incorrect for any future order-aware client.

I don't see this issue addressed by a subsequent patch in this
series.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that nfsd4_encode_fattr4_fsid() calls fsid_source(args->fhp) without checking if args.fhp is NULL, which could lead to a dereference of a NULL pointer in a future CB_NOTIFY attrset that adds FSID.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nit: here, "args.fhp" is never false.

Note that nfsd4_encode_fattr4_fsid() calls fsid_source(args->fhp)
without a NULL check. After this patch is applied, filehandle
encoding is svc_fh-free but FSID encoding is not, and the two
share the same attrmask gate:

   (FATTR4_WORD0_FILEHANDLE | FATTR4_WORD0_FSID).

No current caller trips it: the CB_NOTIFY path does not request
FSID. But a future CB_NOTIFY attrset that adds FSID would
dereference a NULL fhp.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that when two clients use exports of the same directory root with different subtree_check settings, nfsd4_file_hash_insert() can reuse the same nfs4_file object, leading to incorrect connectability in CB_NOTIFY child filehandles. He suggested using the delegation's sc_export to derive connectability from, which is already available via dp->dl_stid.sc_export.
review_comments.raw_body:
When two clients use exports of the same directory root that
differ only in subtree_check/no_subtree_check, the root filehandle
is the same and nfsd4_file_hash_insert() can reuse the same
nfs4_file. This makes fi_connectable depend on whichever export
first initialized the shared object, so a later directory
delegation acquired under the other export can encode CB_NOTIFY
child filehandles with the wrong connectability.

Therefore, the delegation's sc_export is the per-export state to
derive connectability from, and the export is already available
via dp->dl_stid.sc_export.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that setup_notify_fhandle() constructs a child FILEHANDLE attribute without calling fh_append_mac(), which can result in a stale or bad handle being sent to the client, especially for exports requiring signed filehandles. The reviewer recommends either signing the constructed handle or suppressing the attribute when necessary.
review_comments.raw_body:
Codex flagged setup_notify_fhandle() for constructing a child FILEHANDLE
attribute without calling fh_append_mac(): for exports with sign_fh,
fh_compose() appends a MAC, and nfsd_set_fh_dentry() rejects every
non-root signed-export handle whose MAC is absent or mismatched, so a
client using the CB_NOTIFY filehandle gets a stale/bad handle. It
recommends signing the constructed handle or suppressing the attribute
when the export requires signed filehandles.

A client that does not receive the FH falls back to a LOOKUP, so
suppression degrades gracefully.

-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that nfsd4_get_dir_delegation_rsize() budgets only a one-word child-attribute bitmap, which can lead to NFS4ERR_RESOURCE/REP_TOO_BIG errors when encoding a two-word bitmap for supported child attributes in word 1. He suggested addressing this issue by increasing the budgeted size.
review_comments.raw_body:
When a client requests any supported child attribute in word 1, this can
make gddr_child_attributes[1] non-zero, so nfsd4_encode_bitmap4() emits a
two-word bitmap. nfsd4_get_dir_delegation_rsize() still budgets only the
old one-word child-attribute bitmap before executing this non-idempotent
op, so a compound near the reply/slot limit can grant a directory
delegation and then fail encoding with NFS4ERR_RESOURCE/REP_TOO_BIG,
leaving the client without the returned stateid.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that after applying this patch, a client requesting NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS would receive the corresponding bit in gddr_notification, but the callback path still only handles add, remove, and rename notifications, leading to an inconsistency where the server grants directory delegation while promising dir-attribute CB_NOTIFYs it cannot send. The reviewer suggested that this bit should not be advertised in this patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Bisectability: After this patch is applied, a client that requests
NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS now gets that bit echoed in gddr_notification,
but the callback path still only maps/encodes add, remove, and rename
notifications (nfsd_notify_to_ignore(), nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(),
and nfsd4_encode_notify_event() have no dir-attr case). That lets the
server grant a directory delegation while promising dir-attribute
CB_NOTIFYs it cannot send until the follow-up support lands, so this
bit should not be advertised in this patch.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that when a client requests NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS, the CB_NOTIFY event queue can fill up to its maximum size of 3 events while the consumer only accepts 2, leading to an overflow path in nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() that recalls the delegation without draining the queue. This causes nfsd4_cb_notify_release() to requeue the same callback indefinitely.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that when xdr_reserve_space() succeeds but nfsd4_encode_dir_attr_change() returns NULL, the function still takes the success path and does not roll back the reserved space or write to *maskp, potentially leading to a garbage hole being transmitted.

The reviewer noted that the function nfs4xdr.c:4297 accesses dp->dl_stid.sc_export without holding a lock on it, which can lead to a race condition where an in-flight CB_NOTIFY callback drops the last export reference while the function is still accessing it.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that an if statement in nfsd4_encode_notify_event() re-checks dl_notify_mask, which is already gated by the same check in its sole caller, and suggested removing this redundant check.
review_comments.raw_body:
When a client requests NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS, the CB_NOTIFY event
queue can fill to NOTIFY4_EVENT_QUEUE_SIZE (3) events while the consumer
only accepts 2 (it reserves a slot for the dir-attr-change entry). The
resulting overflow path in nfsd4_cb_notify_prepare() recalls the
delegation without draining the queue, and nfsd4_cb_notify_release()
then requeues the same callback indefinitely.

---

Nit:

When xdr_reserve_space() for maskp succeeds but nfsd4_encode_dir_attr_change()
returns NULL, the 4-byte reservation is never rolled back and *maskp is never
written, yet the function still takes the success path (return true). Unlike
the child-event loop, this branch does not escalate to error = true.

This is probably benign only because nfs4_xdr_enc_cb_notify re-encodes from
the ncn_nf[] array (and count was not incremented), so the garbage hole is
never transmitted.

---

The function dereferences the new exp parameter unconditionally.

The argument is dp->dl_stid.sc_export, read unlocked at
nfs4xdr.c:4297 and handed down. An in-flight CB_NOTIFY callback holds a
sc_count reference but NOT an export reference. drop_stid_export() can
run concurrently (admin revoke / unexport), NULL sc_export, and drop what
may be the last export reference, freeing the svc_export while the
callback dereferences it.

---

It looks like this if() re-checks dl_notify_mask even though its
sole caller already gated on the identical check.

nfsd4_encode_notify_event() does not repeat its caller's check.
The guard is unreachable from current callers.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that nfsd_break_one_deleg() needs a refcount_inc_not_zero() to prevent racing recall and lease teardown, agreeing to make the change.
review_comments.raw_body:
This wart has been there a long time, and we just papered over it with
the lock.

I think we need to do a refcount_inc_not_zero() in
nfsd_break_one_deleg() and just return without queuing the callback if
it's already at 0. That means that the recall is racing with the lease
teardown, so I think the right thing to do is to not send the recall in
that case.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
